---
title: "The AD core"
subtitle: "T2 · Phase T2.1 · `va-codegen::ad`"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status: code complete, real coverage.** 10 tests, all green — forward-mode dual numbers over
the `va-ir` expression arena, every differentiated operator checked against a central finite
difference (`CLAUDE.md` §5, non-negotiable).
:::

## Goal

Evaluate an `ExprId` at an operating point *and* differentiate it with respect to every active
unknown, in one pass — the Jacobian a Newton solve needs, computed exactly, not approximated.

## Where it fits

```
 va_ir::Module ──► [ad::eval] ──Dual (value + gradient)──► lower.rs's stamps ──► ModelInstance
```

Every device's `load` call, for every node/branch in the circuit, walks the IR arena through
`ad::eval` to get both the residual contribution and its exact Jacobian row/column in one shot.

## The idea

**Forward-mode, dense gradient vector.** A `Dual` carries its primal `value` plus one partial
derivative per *active unknown* — every node voltage, plus one per branch that has its own
auxiliary current unknown. A potential probe `V(p, n)` seeds `+1` in slot `p` and `-1` in slot
`n`; every other operator (`+`, `*`, `exp`, …) propagates gradients through the ordinary chain
rule. This is the simplest AD mode that's still exact for this problem shape: a compact model
has few unknowns (a handful of terminals/internal nodes) and one scalar output per stamp row, so
forward mode's "one pass per output, dense over all inputs" cost profile is exactly right —
reverse mode would only pay off with many inputs and few outputs, the opposite shape.

**Why exactness is non-negotiable, not just nice-to-have** (`CLAUDE.md` §5): Newton's quadratic
convergence depends on the Jacobian being the *true* derivative of the residual. A subtly wrong
gradient doesn't crash — it silently degrades convergence to something that looks like a
numerics or convergence-aid problem, which is far harder to debug than a wrong value. Every
operator this module implements therefore ships with its own finite-difference test, not just
the ones that "seemed tricky."

**`ddx(expr, probe)` reads the gradient the AD pass already carries**, rather than needing a
second differentiation pass: since every potential probe already seeds a `+1`/`-1` in its node's
own gradient slot, the partial derivative the LRM's `ddx` operator asks for is *already sitting
in* the evaluated `Dual`'s gradient vector at that slot — `eval` just reads it out instead of
re-differentiating from scratch. Validated against the LRM's own worked examples (a VCCS, a
diode), the diode case additionally cross-checked against a central finite difference.

## The code

```rust
pub struct Dual {
    pub value: f64,
    pub grad: Vec<f64>,   // one entry per active unknown, node-slot order
}

impl Dual {
    pub fn variable(value: f64, i: usize, n: usize) -> Self { /* unit derivative in slot i */ }
    pub fn add(&self, o: &Dual) -> Dual { /* (a+b)' = a' + b' */ }
    // ... scale, sub, mul, div, and every unary/binary builtin, each with its exact chain rule
}

pub fn eval(ctx: &Ctx, expr: ExprId) -> Result<Dual, CodegenError>
```

`Ctx` bundles everything `eval` needs to resolve a name against the current operating point:
the module's own arena, parameter values, the solution vector `x`, the active terminal-to-slot
mapping, and (for `ddt`/`idt`) the transient companion state.

## It works

```console
$ cargo test -p va-codegen ad::
running 10 tests
test ad::tests::exp_chain_rule ... ok
test ad::tests::div_matches_finite_difference ... ok
test ad::tests::ddx_of_diode_conductance_matches_finite_difference ... ok
test ad::tests::select_evaluates_only_the_taken_branch ... ok
test ad::tests::vt_of_temperature_scales_and_carries_gradient ... ok
...
test result: ok. 10 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 43 filtered out; finished in 0.00s
```

`select_evaluates_only_the_taken_branch` matters beyond correctness: the ternary's untaken arm
may be genuinely undefined at the current point (`x > 0 ? ln(x) : 0`), so `eval` must never
touch it — the same rule `GeneratedModel::run`'s `if`/`else` lowering (`t2-codegen/02-lowering.qmd`)
follows one level up.

## Limitations

Second derivatives are out of scope: `ddx`'s result is treated as having zero further gradient,
a single-pass, first-order AD design decision, not an oversight. `ddx` is only meaningful against
a *potential* probe — differentiating with respect to a branch flow would need flow probes to be
independent unknowns, which they are not in this codegen, so `ddx` against a flow probe is a
rejected construct, not a silently wrong answer.

## What's next

`t2-codegen/02-lowering.qmd` — using this AD core to build a real `ModelInstance` from an IR
module.
